Note types: character notes (backstory, motivation, physical description, voice examples); world-building notes (locations, rules of the world, historical events); plot notes (story events, cause-and-effect chains); theme notes (the meaning the story explores); reference notes (research for historical accuracy or technical details). Linking structure: character notes link to scenes they appear in; theme notes link to character notes that embody or contradict the theme; world-building notes link to scenes where elements are introduced. The plot structure emerges from the link graph: following the chain from setting notes → character notes → conflict notes → resolution notes creates a scene-by-scene outline. UNSAID: the Zettelkasten approach is better suited for complex, long-form fiction (novels, series) than for short stories — the overhead of building a note network only pays off at sufficient complexity and length.

The 'outboard brain' metaphor (coined by Cory Doctorow) for digital note-taking makes explicit what the PKM movement later systematized: the human brain is the processor, not the storage. Storing in a trusted external system is not an intellectual shortcut — it's an appropriate use of available tools that frees the processor (brain) for processing work. The distinction with the 'second brain' metaphor: 'outboard' implies an extension of existing capacity, not a replacement; 'second brain' implies duplication, which can be misread as the external system doing the thinking. The practical implications: use the outboard brain for everything worth storing (no filter at capture time); accept that most stored items will never be retrieved (storage is cheap); invest effort in retrieval systems (search, links, review) rather than capture organization (the bottleneck is finding, not storing). This framing removes the guilt of 'I have too many notes' — storage is not the problem; retrieval design is.

The 'morning pages' adapted for PKM: Julia Cameron's 3 pages of longhand free-writing, adapted to digital as an unstructured daily note entry with no editing, no backspace, no judgment. Capture whatever is on your mind for 15 minutes. Purpose: clear mental RAM of anxieties, half-formed ideas, and background processing before focused work. PKM integration: morning pages are NOT processed into permanent notes — they're cathartic raw output that occasionally contains insights worth extracting. After writing, scan quickly for any insight worth saving (1-3 sentences at most); everything else stays as is or gets archived. UNSAID: the original morning pages practice explicitly prohibits re-reading morning pages for months after writing, to avoid inner critic paralysis. In PKM terms: morning pages go to archive immediately after the scan, not to inbox. Treating morning pages as PKM content to be processed defeats their purpose — they're cognitive hygiene, not knowledge production.

The 'link as recommendation' problem in Zettelkasten: when every wikilink looks identical, there's no semantic distinction between 'this note is directly related' and 'I happened to mention this concept in passing.' High-value links (this note builds on that one; this note contradicts that one; this note is an example of that concept) are visually indistinguishable from low-value links (I used the word machine learning in a sentence). The constraint: standard markdown wikilinks carry no semantic metadata. Mitigations: (1) use explicit relationship language in the text before the link ('This is a specific example of [[general principle]]' vs. just using [[general principle]] mid-sentence); (2) maintain a separate 'connection note' that explicitly documents the relationship between two notes; (3) use link types if your tool supports them (Logseq's property-based connections); (4) accept the limitation and rely on text context around links for semantic disambiguation.

PKM onboarding for new hires in a company using Obsidian shared vault: (1) Share vault via private GitHub repository with new hire's read access; (2) 'First week' guide note walks through vault structure, key MOCs, and important policies; (3) Assign a 'knowledge buddy' — existing team member who shows new hire how to navigate and contribute; (4) First contribution task: new hire documents one thing they learned in week 1 that wasn't in the vault; (5) Weekly contribution review: manager acknowledges new contributions in team meeting; (6) 30-day review: new hire provides feedback on what was missing, confusing, or outdated — this is the highest-value time for documentation improvement, as fresh eyes spot gaps that insiders overlook. UNSAID: new hire documentation feedback has a 30-day window of maximum value — after 30 days, new hires have adapted to gaps and no longer notice them. Capture this feedback systematically before it's lost.

The 'note retirement' workflow for keeping PKM relevant: instead of deleting obsolete notes, retire them to archive with a `retired: YYYY-MM-DD` frontmatter property and `retirement-reason: [superseded/outdated/irrelevant]`. Retire rather than delete: (1) retired notes preserve the history of your thinking evolution; (2) git history shows when you believed something different; (3) retired notes may be relevant again in different contexts. Retirement triggers: (1) a note directly contradicts a newer, better-evidenced note; (2) a procedure note is for a tool you no longer use; (3) a project note for a project 2+ years closed. Retirement process: add `retired: YYYY-MM-DD` to frontmatter; add `[[superseded-by::[[new note]]]]` link if replaced; move to Archives/ folder. Dataview query to review retirements: `TABLE retired, retirement-reason FROM '' WHERE retired` shows all retired notes for periodic review (some may need re-activation). UNSAID: never auto-retire based on age alone — an old note may be the most important in your vault. Retirement is a human judgment call.

NEVER migrate PKM tools during high-pressure periods (job change, major project crunch, life transitions). The failure: someone decides their Evernote system is insufficient, begins migrating to Obsidian during a project crunch, spending 20 hours on migration instead of the project, while having no reliable system during the transition. PKM migration has a temporary productivity dip: the new system is unfamiliar, the old system is abandoned, and for 2-6 weeks you have no trusted PKM system. The correct timing for PKM migration: between major projects or at natural transition points (new year, end of a project phase). The correct migration approach: run both systems in parallel for 2-4 weeks (capture to new, keep old as reference) before fully switching. This parallel period reveals the real trade-offs of the new system before you're fully committed. Sunk cost fallacy: staying in an inadequate PKM because you've invested time in it is less harmful than migrating at the wrong time.

The embodied cognition perspective on PKM: physical manipulation of ideas (writing by hand, moving index cards, drawing diagrams) engages different cognitive processes than digital manipulation. The act of physically writing a note on an index card and placing it near a related card is not just storage — it's a spatially-mediated thinking process. Digital tools compensate with other affordances (search, links, backlinks) but lose the embodied manipulation. This is why some knowledge workers maintain a hybrid system: digital for storage and retrieval, physical for active thinking and synthesis. The 'thinking desk' pattern: during synthesis or writing sessions, print out 5-10 related notes and arrange them physically to see relationships that are harder to perceive when switching between windows. The limitation: printed notes are not linked and cannot be dynamically queried. The physical arrangement is for thinking, not for permanent organization.

Obsidian Note Refactor plugin for splitting complex notes: goes further than Note Composer by providing bulk operations. Use case: a long meeting minutes document with 5 decision discussions → extract each discussion as a separate decision note. Workflow: add 'split marker' headings (`## Split`) to indicate where to split; run 'Extract selection to new note' for each section; resulting notes are created with the heading as filename and linked back to the original. For structured note creation from templates: 'Create note from template' generates a new note with a pre-populated template and places the link in the current note. UNSAID: splitting a large note into many small notes requires a commitment to maintaining the new small notes — splitting is only the first step. Each new note should be reviewed, linked to related notes, and have appropriate metadata added. Splitting without subsequent linking produces small isolated notes that are harder to find than the original large note.

The 'connection overload' problem in dense Zettelkasten networks: when a permanent note has 20+ incoming links, the backlinks panel becomes overwhelming and loses its value as a navigation aid. Notes that are highly connected (hub notes) are typically concept notes for fundamental ideas ('feedback loop', 'system thinking', 'causality') that are referenced everywhere — but the value of seeing all 50 backlinks to 'feedback loop' is low because most links are incidental mentions. The constraint: link density has a quality ceiling beyond which additional links reduce navigation value. Mitigations: (1) distinguish strong links (this note directly builds on that note) from weak links (that concept is mentioned in this note) using text proximity; (2) use MOCs to curate the most important backlinks for hub notes; (3) accept that hub notes are navigational entry points, not traversal endpoints — use them to navigate to more specific notes.

Tana as a structured PKM alternative: Tana uses a 'supertag' system where note types (Books, People, Projects, Concepts) have defined schemas with custom fields and views. Unlike Notion's database model, Tana embeds structure directly in the outline — any node in the outline can be tagged as a type. Advantages over Obsidian: native structured data (no plugin required); AI-assisted note creation (Tana AI suggests type and fields); real-time search with field filtering; built-in collaboration. Disadvantages: proprietary cloud storage; markdown export has limitations; relatively young product with active API changes; higher learning curve than basic Obsidian. Best fit: power users who want structured data in a linked outliner, are comfortable with cloud storage, and don't require plugin extensibility. UNSAID: Tana's 'supertag' innovation is significant but the product is in active development — early adopters accept incomplete features and breaking changes as the tradeoff for being on the cutting edge.

NEVER use folder structure as a proxy for understanding. The failure: creating deep, elaborate folder hierarchies as a substitute for actually thinking about how ideas relate. A vault with 10 levels of folders and 50 notes per leaf folder contains 'organized' but unconnected notes — the folder structure creates an illusion of understanding ('I know where my machine learning notes are') without the knowledge network that enables synthesis. Folders organize by category; links organize by relationship. Categories don't think; relationships do. The diagnostic: if you can sort your notes into folders without reading them (based on title and tag alone), the organizational work required no understanding. If you can link two notes meaningfully, you had to understand both well enough to see their relationship. The effort invested in understanding is captured in links, not in folder structure.

Building a team 'decision log' using shared Obsidian or Confluence: (1) Create a Decision Log template: decision title, date, decision makers, context (what problem), options considered, decision made, rationale, expected outcomes, review date; (2) Store all significant decisions (architecture, strategy, process, product) in the log; (3) Link decisions to relevant project notes and affected team members; (4) Review log at retrospectives: how did expected outcomes compare to actual outcomes? (5) When a decision is revisited, create a new 'Decision Revision' note with link to the original decision — never delete or overwrite old decisions; (6) Tag by domain: `#decision/architecture`, `#decision/product`, `#decision/process`. UNSAID: the decision log is most valuable when the original decision-maker has left the team — it preserves the reasoning that would otherwise require reconstructing through email archaeology. Teams that maintain decision logs spend significantly less time re-litigating past decisions.

The 'lazy linking' practice for building note connections during browsing: when reading a note, don't plan to add links — instead, whenever you instinctively think 'this reminds me of X', create the wikilink to X immediately. The link is created by the associative thought itself, not by deliberate analysis. Benefits: (1) links capture actual cognitive associations rather than theoretical taxonomic relationships; (2) no separate 'linking session' required — links accumulate during normal reading; (3) surprises reveal unexpected connections your conscious categorization would miss. The practice is 'lazy' because it requires no planning: just follow the impulse to link whenever a connection comes to mind. Over 6 months of lazy linking, hub notes (notes with many incoming links) reveal your actual cognitive anchors — the concepts you consistently associate with other concepts. UNSAID: lazy linking produces some low-quality links (incidental word associations), but the signal-to-noise ratio is much better than not linking at all.

The epistemological foundation of PKM: notes are not facts, they are claims — and claims have varying levels of confidence. A literature note claiming 'the author argues X' is different from a permanent note claiming 'X is true.' A speculation note claiming 'I hypothesize X' is different from both. PKM systems that treat all notes as equally authoritative produce a knowledge base with no epistemic hygiene. The practical implication: using confidence metadata (`confidence: high/medium/low/speculative`) on notes signals how much to trust the claim before using it in decisions or sharing with others. Andy Matuschak's notes use visual density (sparse = exploratory, dense = well-developed) as an implicit confidence signal. The most rigorous approach: literature notes cite sources; permanent notes cite literature notes; speculative notes are clearly marked as hypotheses. This epistemic chain enables tracing any claim back to its evidence.

Remote Obsidian vault access via self-hosted Gitea + Obsidian Git on mobile: (1) Set up Gitea on a VPS (self-hosted GitHub alternative, < 1GB RAM); (2) Push vault to Gitea private repository; (3) Configure Obsidian Git on iOS/Android to pull from Gitea; (4) Access vault anywhere with internet. Cost: ~$5/month VPS. Complete data ownership alternative to Obsidian Sync ($10/month) or iCloud (often unreliable for vaults). The technical stack: Gitea runs on the VPS; Obsidian Git handles pull/push on mobile; a daily cron job on the VPS backs up the Gitea repository to S3 for disaster recovery. UNSAID: Gitea requires server maintenance (updates, security patches) — if you're not comfortable with VPS management, Obsidian Sync or GitHub private repository is a better tradeoff. GitHub free private repositories are an even simpler alternative with no server management cost; the only limitation is GitHub's Terms of Service regarding the vault content.

The 'note titling' constraint in Zettelkasten: good permanent note titles are the most difficult and most important part of the system. A well-titled note is: (1) self-explanatory without context ('L2 regularization prevents overfitting by penalizing large weights' vs. 'Regularization notes'); (2) makes a single claim ('Constraint X causes behavior Y') rather than naming a topic ('About X'); (3) specific enough to distinguish from similar notes ('Scheduled review outperforms cramming for long-term retention' vs. 'Study techniques'); (4) short enough to be a wikilink without truncation (< 70 characters). The constraint: titling takes significant effort and often requires understanding the concept well enough to summarize it in one sentence — which is exactly the point. If you can't title a permanent note concisely and specifically, you don't yet understand the idea well enough to write it as a permanent note.

Obsidian vault migration to a new computer: (1) Ensure all changes are committed and pushed to git remote (Obsidian Git: 'Commit all changes' + 'Push'); (2) On new computer: install Obsidian; install git; clone vault: `git clone https://github.com/username/vault.git ~/Documents/MyVault`; (3) Open vault in Obsidian: 'Open Folder as Vault'; (4) Install plugins: Obsidian remembers plugin list in `.obsidian/plugins/` (committed to git) — but plugin binaries are NOT committed; run 'Community Plugins → Browse → Enable' for each required plugin, or use the Obsidian42 BRAT plugin to bulk install; (5) Verify Obsidian Git settings: pull interval, push interval, author name/email; (6) Test round-trip: create a test note, commit, push, verify it appears in the git remote. Time estimate: 30-45 minutes including plugin reinstall. UNSAID: if `.obsidian/` is not committed to git (some users gitignore it), all plugin settings, hotkeys, and theme configuration must be manually reconfigured on the new machine.

The 'curiosity-driven linking' strategy for building a high-value note network: when adding a link, pause and ask 'why am I linking these two notes?' The answer reveals whether the link is meaningful. High-value link reasons: 'this is a specific example of that general principle' (instantiation); 'this note directly contradicts that note' (tension); 'this note builds on that note's foundation' (elaboration); 'these two notes together produce a new insight neither has alone' (synthesis). Low-value link reasons: 'both notes mention the same word' (keyword overlap); 'both notes are about the same topic area' (categorical proximity). Documenting link rationale: add a brief inline note after the link: `[[L2 regularization]] (contrasts with [[dropout]] — L2 is parametric penalty, dropout is stochastic noise)`. This annotation makes the link semantically meaningful and guides future readers through the intended connection. UNSAID: annotated links take 3x longer to create than bare links but produce 10x more navigation value — invest the extra effort for the most important connections.

Obsidian's vault is a directory of markdown files — this means standard command-line tools (grep, find, sed, awk) work on vault content without any special API. The constraint is also a capability: `grep -r 'spaced repetition' ~/vault/` finds all notes mentioning spaced repetition; `find ~/vault/ -newer ~/vault/.lastrun -name '*.md'` finds notes modified since a timestamp; `wc -l ~/vault/**/*.md` counts total lines across all notes. This filesystem transparency enables custom automation that Obsidian's plugin API cannot: batch rename via `rename`; cross-vault analysis; custom export pipelines. The constraint: direct file manipulation outside Obsidian must respect Obsidian's link format and avoid breaking wikilinks. Renaming a file externally requires finding and updating all `[[old-name]]` references — do this with Obsidian open (it handles rename) or via a comprehensive find-and-replace script that handles all link variants (`[[Old Name]]`, `[[Old Name|alias]]`, `![[Old Name]]`).

NEVER treat PKM as a destination rather than a means. The failure: spending 2 hours per day maintaining a beautiful Zettelkasten with no corresponding increase in the quality or quantity of thinking, writing, or decisions. The PKM has become the goal rather than the tool. Signs: optimizing for note count metrics; focusing on link graph aesthetics; writing notes about PKM methodology without using the PKM for other topics; sharing PKM screenshots on social media as evidence of productivity. The test: name the last important decision your PKM influenced. Name the last essay or project your PKM materially contributed to. If neither comes to mind, the PKM is a hobby, not a tool. Hobbies are valid, but they should be recognized as such rather than justified as productivity infrastructure. Recalibration: set a goal ('I will write one essay per month using only PKM content') and measure PKM value by progress toward the goal, not by system quality metrics.

Obsidian + Templater + Python for a custom import pipeline: Python script watches an inbox directory (watchdog library), processes new files (PDF → text via pdfplumber, HTML → markdown via markdownify, audio → text via Whisper), applies naming conventions, populates frontmatter from extracted metadata, and deposits into Obsidian vault inbox folder. Templater post-processing: when Obsidian sees a new file in inbox, Templater auto-applies a template that prompts for tags and personal commentary. The pipeline converts heterogeneous input (PDFs, web clips, voice memos) into consistently formatted Obsidian notes without manual format conversion. File format conversion commands: `pdftomk article.pdf | markdownify > article.md; whisper audio.m4a --output_format txt > voice.txt`. UNSAID: custom import pipelines have high initial setup cost (2-5 hours) but low ongoing maintenance if sources are stable. Source format changes (new website CSS, different audio recorder) require pipeline updates — plan for periodic maintenance.

Personal knowledge management at scale (1000+ notes) requires fundamentally different practices than at small scale (< 200 notes). At small scale: you can remember what you have; browsing reveals all relevant notes; any organization system works because the total cognitive overhead is low. At large scale: memory of vault contents fails; browsing is impractical; poor organization compounds into major retrieval failures; link density determines navigation quality. The inflection point (300-500 notes) is where many PKM practitioners give up — the system has grown beyond what intuitive navigation can handle, but before the practices that make large-scale PKM work (consistent MOC maintenance, systematic linking, regular review) have been established. The prescription: adopt large-scale practices before you need them (start MOCs at note 50, not note 500). Building practices while the system is still manageable is vastly easier than retrofitting them onto an overwhelming backlog.

Annual PKM retrospective procedure: (1) Quantitative review: how many permanent notes created? How many books processed? How many outputs (essays, decisions, projects) supported by PKM? (2) Qualitative review: what is the most valuable insight you captured this year? What topic cluster grew most? What decision was best informed by PKM? (3) System review: which workflows were actually followed vs. aspirational? Which plugins added genuine value? (4) Goal setting for next year: one quantitative goal (e.g., 100 permanent notes); one qualitative goal (e.g., publish one essay per month from PKM); one system improvement (e.g., implement weekly review consistently); (5) Write the retrospective as a permanent note — it becomes a historical record of your PKM philosophy evolution; (6) Read last year's retrospective before writing this year's — measure progress against previous goals. UNSAID: the retrospective is the mechanism that prevents the PKM system from becoming a treadmill of activity with no reflection on effectiveness. Without retrospectives, the same inefficiencies persist indefinitely.
